summary: Gruber examines the updated iMessage editing and un-sending features in the fourth iOS 16 and macOS 13 developer betas. The edit feature now allows 15 minutes and up to five edits with visible edit history, while unsending has a two-minute window. He notes the backwards-compatibility problem where older OS versions display edits as new messages and unsent messages remain visible. Gruber defends each design decision—time limits, edit history, and edit caps—as thoughtful balances between sender convenience and recipient rights. He predicts both features will be widely used once adoption catches up.
key_points: Message editing now has a 15-minute window, five-edit cap, and visible edit history for recipients—a significant tightening from earlier betas
Unsending has a two-minute limit and leaves a trace on the recipient's device, but isn't backwards-compatible with older OS versions
Edit history exists because once a message arrives on a recipient's device, they have an implied property right to see what was sent to them
Editing and unsending serve distinct purposes: editing fixes typos in-place even mid-conversation, while unsending means the message shouldn't have been sent at all
The backwards-compatibility gap is an unfortunate but temporary problem that will resolve as OS adoption increases
money_quotes: Once the original typo-laden message arrives on the recipient's device, it no longer belongs solely to the sender.
There's an implied property right, as it were, for the recipient to be able to see what was sent to them.
A malicious actor can't send an abusive or misleading message and then edit it without a paper trail.
Why limit edits to five changes? Because stop screwing around.
Five seems generous, really — my gut feeling is three strikes and you're done.
Why have time limits? Because ink needs to dry.
Undo Send means 'it was a mistake to send this message at all.'
key_insight: iMessage's editing and unsending features are thoughtfully designed around the principle that once a message is delivered, the recipient has a right to know what was sent, making edit history and time limits necessary guardrails rather than arbitrary restrictions.
